css--docs.name: Docs
css--docs.title: css
css--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as CSSVariables from "../tokens/Tokens.stories";

<Meta title="CSS" />;

# CSS

Before continuing, please read the page [About design tokens](https://internal.qlik.dev/design/sprout/design-tokens/about-design-tokens/) to understand the core concepts of Sprout Design System tokens.
You will also find the great documentation page [list of all tokens](https://internal.qlik.dev/design/sprout/design-tokens/list-of-all-tokens/).

Our implementation provide two levels of possible usage. The first one or low level is using raw css relying on CSS Variables listed below.
The second one is using css modules with a great API inspired by tailwindcss.

- [All tokens as CSS Variables](?path=/story/css-tokens--data-table) to use in raw CSS
- [@qlik/sprout-css-modules](?path=/docs/css-sprout-css-modules--docs) to use in any framework inspired by tailwindcss
- [@qlik/sprout-react](?path=/docs/css-sprout-css-modules--docs#qlik-trialsprout-react-classnames) classNames to use in React

components-overview--docs.name: Docs
components-overview--docs.title: Components/Overview
components-overview--docs.content:
import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; # Components This document specifies all common APIs and patterns used across all components in this package. ## Component API Each component in the library is designed to closely align with the native behavior of the web platform. The goal is to avoid inventing new APIs and instead adhere as much as possible to web standards. Since this component library is an implementation of a design system, we do not allow `className` and `style` injection. **Customization** of components is considered harmful. To customize the look and feel, you should refer to [Design Tokens](?path=/docs/docs-designtokens--docs) or [OEM](?path=/docs/docs-oem--docs). These tools are intended for OEM users, not frontend developers using Sprout. The design system provides "lego bricks" for building consistent UIs. If the style does not match the design, it could mean one of two things: - The designer has created a new component. In this case, the frontend developer should either create the new component or challenge the design. - The designer has modified (detached or overridden) the component. This is considered a harmful practice and should be strongly challenged. The component API should follow Figma properties whenever possible. For example, a `State='disabled'` in Figma should correspond to the `:disabled` pseudo-class in CSS. Sometimes, a property in Figma may be designer-oriented, such as `hasIcon`. As a frontend developer, you should translate this into an `icon` property. Each component should expose its property definitions in TypeScript using the convention `[ComponentName]Props`. ```typescript export type MyComponentProps = HTMLDivProps & { figmaProp: boolean; icon: ReactNode; }; ``` ## Spread the Rest of the Properties to the Highest Element in the Tree ```typescript
rest of the code
``` Spreading props in a component library is useful to allow users to make small adjustments, such as adding `data-attributes`. ## Controlled / Uncontrolled This [state pattern](https://react.dev/learn/sharing-state-between-components#controlled-and-uncontrolled-components) is primarily applied to form inputs in the React documentation. However, the concept extends to any component that provides interactions. The key decision is whether the parent component or the provided component manages the state. A component library should support both controlled and uncontrolled behaviors. However, implementing both can be cumbersome, as the code becomes harder to write and read. To simplify this, you can use library like [uncontrollable](https://www.npmjs.com/package/uncontrollable). ## Accessibility This implementation follows the [WAI-ARIA](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/aria/) guidelines and relies on the [ARIA Authoring Practices Guide](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/) to ensure compliance with [WCAG](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/) AA standards. Since the library uses many native HTML elements, components provide their **implicit** roles and states. Each component is tested using the [axe-core](https://www.deque.com/axe/) library across all possible states. Storybook is set up with the `@storybook/addon-a11y` plugin, allowing anyone to view accessibility reports for each story. We also test keyboard navigation using Playwright to ensure that gestures remain functional after changes. An important note: this implementation stays as native as possible to the browser. It does not attempt to fix browser-specific issues that deviate from the spec. We believe browsers are evolving every day, and users are familiar with their browser's behavior. For example, a button will not display the `:active` state when the user presses the space key in Firefox. This is due to [Bug 68851](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=68851), reported 24 years ago, which explains that Firefox does not trigger the `:active` state because the spec defines the "main" action as a `click`. In contrast, Chrome triggers the `:active` state even on a right-click. Some open-source libraries, such as Adobe's [react-spectrum](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com), reimplement everything in JavaScript (event handlers) to create state machines for every component. For example, the `react-aria` button in Firefox will display the `:active` state, but this comes at the cost of significant JavaScript overhead. ## Responsive Design Currently, we rely on CSS media queries to handle responsive design. We do not provide any utilities for this purpose because we are a component library, not a CSS framework. Our goal is to avoid reinventing the wheel by creating a new API for responsive design. We believe CSS media queries are the most effective way to manage responsive design in a component library. It is worth noting that media queries do not support CSS variables. We have explored alternative solutions, such as JavaScript-based approaches, but they did not gain sufficient traction. Within `@qlik/sprout-css-modules`, we intentionally avoid using prefixed CSS classes, such as those used by TailwindCSS. This is because TailwindCSS operates as a compile-time utility, whereas our library functions at runtime. In conclusion, the current recommendation is to use CSS media queries in projects that utilize Sprout.

components-avatar--docs.name: Docs
components-avatar--docs.title: Components/Avatar
components-avatar--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Avatar.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Avatar" />

# Avatar

Avatars represent a user in the system.

```tsx
import { Avatar, type AvatarProps } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<Avatar size="s" text="OC" />

<Avatar img={{src: 'http://', alt: 'img title'}} />

<Avtar icon={<MyIcon />}>
```

## Text

<Canvas of={Stories.TextStory} />
<Controls of={Stories.TextStory} />

## Icon

<Canvas of={Stories.Icon} />
<Controls of={Stories.Icon} />

## Image

<Canvas of={Stories.Img} />
<Controls of={Stories.Img} />

## Accessibility

HTML structure

- The component use a simple `span` tag with the `role="img"` attribute.
- The `aria-label` property or `aria-labelledby` must be provided for accessibility reason.
- The `img` property if provided must contains an `alt` attribute for accessibility reason.

components-breadcrumb--docs.name: Docs
components-breadcrumb--docs.title: Components/Breadcrumb
components-breadcrumb--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Breadcrumb.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Breadcrumb" />

# Breadcrumb

A breadcrumb is a navigational UI for hierarchical content.
The `Breadcrumb` component extends the native [HTML nav element](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/nav) attributes, so all standard navigation landmark properties are supported.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Breadcrumb, BreadcrumbItem, Link } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<Breadcrumb
  items={[
    {
      label: "Root",
      href: "/",
    },
    {
      label: "Folder",
      href: "/folder",
    },
    {
      label: "Page",
      href: "/page",
    },
  ]}
/>

// Both examples are equivalent and valid but note that you need
// to add the `aria-current="page"` attribute on the last link.

<Breadcrumb>
    <BreadcrumbItem>
      <Link href="/">Home</Link>
    </BreadcrumbItem>
    <BreadcrumbItem>
      <Link href="/folder">Folder</Link>
    </BreadcrumbItem>
    <BreadcrumbItem>
      <Link href="/folder/mypage" aria-current="page">
        current page
      </Link>
    </BreadcrumbItem>
</Breadcrumb>
```

## Properties

<Canvas of={Stories.Basic} />
<Controls of={Stories.Basic} />

## Accessibility

The `composition` API doesn't add the needed attribute to respect the accessibility spec, so please consider that great powers comes with responsibilities.

[W3/WAI/ARIA/APG Breadcrumb](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/)

The current component use the `Link` component so refer to it for the corresponding accessibility questions.

- The set of Links is structured using an ordered list.
- The Breadcrumb (nav element) is labeled Breadcrumb identifies the structure as a breadcrumb trail and makes it a navigation landmark so that it is easy to locate.
- The Link to the current page has aria-current set to page. If the element representing the current page is not a link, aria-current is optional.

components-checkbox--docs.name: Docs
components-checkbox--docs.title: Components/Checkbox
components-checkbox--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Checkbox.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Checkbox" />

# Checkbox

Checkboxes are form elements that allows a user to select one or multiple options from a list. Checkboxes can also be used to turn on and off controls in an interface.

## Usage

```jsx
import { Checkbox, CheckboxGroup } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<Checkbox
  name="myfield"
  label="Label"
  helpText="help me"
  checked
  disabled
  onChange={() => {}}
/>;

<CheckboxGroup label="Select options">
  <Checkbox name="option1" label="Option 1" />
  <Checkbox name="option2" label="Option 2" checked />
  <Checkbox name="option3" label="Option 3" disabled />
</CheckboxGroup>;

<CheckboxGroup
  label="Select options"
  options={[
    { name: "option1", label: "Option 1" },
    { name: "option2", label: "Option 2", checked: true },
    { name: "option3", label: "Option 3", disabled: true },
  ]}
/>;
```

## Properties

The Checkbox directly inherits properties from [HTML input element](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/input/checkbox), excluding `value`, `type`, and `prefix`.

### Checkbox

<Canvas of={Stories.Playground} />
<Controls of={Stories.Playground} />

### CheckboxGroup

<Canvas of={Stories.Group} />
<Controls of={Stories.Group} />

## Accessibility

[W3/WAI/ARIA/APG Checkbox](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/checkbox/)

HTML Structure

The current component use a native `input` tag using the attribute type set to `checkbox`. It means we do not need aria attributes except for the tri-state to express the `mixed` value.

- Checkbox has the [implicit role](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/input#technical_summary) `checkbox`.
- Checkbox support all [accessible label](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/forms/labels/) using `label` props for example.
- Checkbox when checked add the `checked` html attribute so it has the implicit corresponding state.

Keyboard

- `Space` change the focused checkbox state to checked if unchecked and vice versa.

import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as GridStories from "./Grid.stories";
import * as GridColumnStories from "./GridColumn.stories";
import * as GridContainerStories from "./GridContainer.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Grid" />

# Grid

Grid is a powerful layout system available as a React component.
It's entirely built on top `@qlik/sprout-css-modules` [grid](/?path=/docs/css-sprout-css-modules--docs#grid) and [media](/?path=/story/css-sprout-css-modules--media)

Sprout defines a 16 based column system that is great to create responsive layouts.

## Grid.Container

<Canvas of={GridContainerStories.GridContainer} />

### API

<Controls of={GridContainerStories.GridContainer} />

## Grid.Column

<Canvas of={GridColumnStories.GridColumn} />
<Controls of={GridColumnStories.GridColumn} />

## Responsive styles

The `useMedia` hook is a React hook that allow you to easily get the current viewport size.

```tsx
import { useMedia } from "@qlik/sprout-react-hooks";
```

<Canvas of={GridStories.ResponsiveStyles} />

components-link--docs.name: Docs
components-link--docs.title: Components/Link
components-link--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Link.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Link" />

# Link

```tsx
import { Link } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<Link href="/folder">Folder</Link>;

<Link href="https://www.qlik.com/" font="inherit" isExternal>
  External link
</Link>;

<Link href="#" withEllipsis isExternal>
  With ellipsis and isExternal too long you will see ellipsis
</Link>;
```

## Demo

<Canvas of={Stories.Playground} />
<Controls of={Stories.Playground} />

## Accessibility

[W3/WAI/ARIA/APG link](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/link/)

HTML structure:

- The current implementation use the native HTML `a` tag so it has the implicit role `link`.
- Use the Link component `disabled` property will have to effect:
  - `aria-disabled` to indicate that the link is not available
  - set `tabindex` to `-1` to remove the link from the tab order

Keyboard Interaction:

`Enter`: Executes the link and moves focus to the link target.
`Shift + F10` on `MS Windows`: Opens a context menu for the link.

components-progress--docs.name: Docs
components-progress--docs.title: Components/Progress
components-progress--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Progress from "./Progress.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Progress" />

# Progress

Progress indicators, or loaders, are used to show progression of system events. They can be determinate or indeterminate.

## ProgressBar

Used for progress representation. ProgressBar directly inherits properties from [HTML div element](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/div) but add some extra properties to handle the progress representation.

### Usage

```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<ProgressBar percent="70" aria-label="work in progress" />;
```

### Properties

<Canvas of={Progress.PlaygroundProgressBar} />
<Controls of={Progress.PlaygroundProgressBar} />

### Accessibility

[MDN ARIA: progressbar role](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/progressbar_role)

HTML Structure

- `ProgressBar` uses the HTML tag `div`
- if the props `percent` is set
  - the role `progressbar` is used.
  - the attributes `aria-value[min|max|now]` are added
- if no `percent` props is passed it is considered as indetermined
  - the role `status` is used.
  - the attributes `aria-busy` are added

## ProgressCircular

Used for progress representation within components, such as a button or a textfield. Can be used by its own. ProgressCircular directly inherits properties from [SVG element](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/SVG/Element/svg) but add some extra properties to handle the progress representation.

### Usage

```tsx
import { ProgressCircular } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<ProgressCircular size="m" percent="70" />;
```

### Properties

<Canvas of={Progress.PlaygroundProgressCircular} />
<Controls of={Progress.PlaygroundProgressCircular} />

### Accessibility

This component is a visual component made using SVG. It rely on the following aria-attributes to describe the progress semantic:

- if no `percent` props: it uses [role="status"](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/status_role)
- if `percent` props: it uses [role="progressbar"](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/progressbar_role)

components-tooltip--docs.name: Docs

components-tooltip--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Tooltip.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Tooltip" />

# Tooltip

Tooltips are contextual helpers that show information about an element when being hovered or focused.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Tooltip } from "@qlik/sprout-react";


<Tooltip title="Tooltip content">
  <button>Hover or focus me</button
</Tooltip>
```

> Note: For performance reason we do not mount the content of the tooltip except if it has an explicit `id`.
> That is due to accessiblity requirement, if there is an id, an other element may refer to it using `aria-labelledby`.

## Properties

<Canvas of={Stories.Playground} />
<Controls of={Stories.Playground} />

## Accessibility

[W3/WAI/ARIA/APG Tooltip](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/tooltip/)

HTML structure

- The element that serves as the tooltip container has role `tooltip`.

Keyboard navigation

- `Escape`: Dismisses the Tooltip.

components-tree--docs.name: Docs
components-tree--docs.title: Components/Tree
components-tree--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as Stories from "./Tree.stories";

<Meta title="Components/Tree" />

# Tree

Tree views help users navigate large amounts of information by organizing content into a hierarchical structure using nested headings.In the tree view, branch nodes can be expanded or collapsed to reveal or hide their child nodes.

```jsx
import { Tree } from "@qlik/sprout-react";

<Tree.TreeItem
  id="leaf-icon"
  path={["folder_expanded"]}
  label="Leaf icon"
  isLeaf
  icon={<FileOutline />}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.TreeItem} />

<Controls of={Stories.TreeItem} />

## Tree in a Table List / Data

<Canvas of={Stories.TreeTable} />

## Accessibility

[W3/WAI/ARIA/APG Tree View](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/treeview/)

HTML Structure

The current component use a native `button` and a `IconButton` to manage the expand state.

Keyboard

- `Space` change the expand state.

table-docs-columns-column-selection--doc.title: Table/Docs/Columns/column selection
table-docs-columns-column-selection--doc.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import * as ListStories from "./ListTable.stories";
import * as DataStories from "./DataTable.stories";

<Meta title="Table/Docs/Columns/column selection" />

# Column selection

Column selection allows users to select one or more columns in a table. This can be useful for various purposes, such as highlighting important data or enabling actions on selected columns.

## Enabling column selection

To enable column selection, you need to set the `columnSelectionMode` prop on the `Table` component. This prop can take two values: `single` or `multiple`.
Then you can push your controlled state into it. To make it easier to use with typescript both states can be pushed but only one will be used:

<Controls of={DataStories.ColumnSelection} />
<Canvas of={DataStories.ColumnSelection} />

table-docs-rows-row-buffer--docs.name: Docs
table-docs-rows-row-buffer--docs.title: Table/Docs/Rows/Row buffer
table-docs-rows-row-buffer--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Table/Docs/Rows/Row buffer" />

# Table - Row buffer

Customize the number of items to render above and below the viewport.
Increasing this number will increase the amount of time it takes to render the virtualizer.

It can be useful to increase and set custom value in case of rendering large tables,
to avoid seeing slow rendering blank items at the top and bottom of the virtualizer when scrolling.
But the bigger the number is the more it will consume resources and make the entire page slower.
A good value should be the number of element visible in your page.

```tsx
import { Table } from "@qlik/sprout-table";

return <Table rows={rows} columns={columns} rowBuffer={50} />;
```

table-docs-layout--docs.name: Docs
table-docs-layout--docs.title: Table/Docs/Layout
table-docs-layout--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { AlertInline, classNames } from "@qlik/sprout-react";
import * as ListStories from "./ListTable.stories";
import * as DataStories from "./DataTable.stories";

<Meta title="Table/Docs/Layout" />

# Table layout

By default, the Table fills the space of its parent container, so that container must have intrinsic dimensions. In other words, if the container has no child elements, then it still must have non-zero dimensions.

<Canvas of={ListStories.LayoutAuto} />

## Flex parent container

The Table can be rendered inside a `flex` container with `flex_column`. Without setting the minimum and maximum height, the Table takes as much space as it needs to display all rows.

<Canvas of={ListStories.FlexParentContainer} />

## Minimum and maximum height

In the demo below, the Table is inside a `flex` container with `flex_column` and `min-height: 200px` and a `max-height: 400px` and adapts its height when the number of rows changes.

<Canvas of={ListStories.FlexParentContainerBoundaries} />

## Predefined dimensions

You can also set predefined dimensions on the parent container. In the demo below, the Table is inside a container with a fixed height of `300px`.

<Canvas of={ListStories.PredefinedDimensions} />

## Percentage dimensions

When using percentages (%) for height or width, make sure that the Table's parent container has intrinsic dimensions. Browsers adjust the element based on a percentage of its parent's size.
If the parent has no size, the percentage will be zero.

table-docs-overlays--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; import * as ListStories from "./ListTable.stories"; import * as DataStories from "./DataTable.stories"; ## loading If the data are fecthed but you have the column definition you can display the Table using the `loading` property. Loading leverage the `renderCellLoading` if defined in the column definition. ```tsx { field: "name", headerName: "Name", flex: 1, renderCellLoading: () => (
), }, ``` ## noRowsOverlay If no rows are passed you will have a default message. You can customize this overlay by using the property `noRowsOverlay`

table-docs-pagination--docs.name: Docs
table-docs-pagination--docs.title: Table/Docs/Pagination
table-docs-pagination--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import * as ListStories from "./ListTable.stories";
import * as DataStories from "./DataTable.stories";

<Meta title="Table/Docs/Pagination" />

## Pagination

Pagination is a technique used to divide large datasets into smaller, more manageable chunks or pages.
In the context of the table component, pagination allows users to navigate through large sets of data without overwhelming the interface or causing performance issues.

In the current implementation, pagination is achieved through a combination using infinite scrolling and traditional pagination controls on touch devices, ensuring a smooth user experience across different devices.
On a touch enabled device, a "Load more" button will appear at the bottom of the table to allow users to load additional rows manually. Otherwise, as the user scrolls down the table, more rows will be automatically loaded when they reach the bottom of the current view.

<Canvas of={ListStories.Pagination} />

<Controls of={ListStories.Pagination} />

css-sprout-css-modules-patterns--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta, Title } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; import * as Stories from "./patterns.stories"; # Patterns Here you will find classNames that doesn't exists in tailwindcss as they are opinionated classes that provide accessibility and useful features for every day use. ## Focusable / Activable blocks It's not rare to see the need to make a `div` focusable to improve user experience. But it's wrong in the accessibility point of view. One solution is to use `tabindex="0"` on the `div` but it's still not enough as you must also provide a click and keydown handler. In the end the goal is to make a `div` behave like a button or a link. To achieve that sprout-css-modules provide some utility classes to help you with that. ### Don't ```tsx
Focusable div yes but NOT ACCESSIBLE SO PLEASE DON'T
``` ### Do ```tsx
``` ## Progressive disclosure [Progressive disclosure](https://internal.qlik.dev/design/sprout/design-patterns/progressive-disclosure/) is a pattern used to show additional content or actions only when needed, reducing cognitive load and improving user experience. ### Display actions on Hover One common request for a dev is to display actions on hover. #### Don't ```css /* Hide actions by default */ .actions { display: none; } /* Show actions on container hover but that will not be shown in keyboard navigation !!! */ .container:hover .actions { display: block; } ``` #### Do The following example demonstrates how to implement progressive disclosure using Sprout CSS Modules: ```tsx
``` ````

my title

// But you can use 'flex' className to reset styles

my title

``` ## Semantic While you can use any class to apply style the semantic of the document stays important for accessibility. Use the right heading level for your titles and subtitles. So order is important and should be defined by the application structure. ### Don't: - skip styles - use `

hooks-getting-started--docs.name: Docs
hooks-getting-started--docs.title: hooks/Getting Started
hooks-getting-started--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="hooks/Getting Started" />

# Getting Started

Welcome to the Sprout CSS Hooks documentation! This guide will help you get started with using the Sprout CSS Hooks library in your React projects.

## Installation

To install the Sprout CSS Hooks library, you can use npm or yarn. Run one of the following commands in your project directory:

```bash
npm install @qlik/sprout-react-hooks
```

## Usage

This package is used by all the react components built in Sprout. It follows semver. The primary target is to share useful internal hooks that we need across multiple repositories.

## Available Hooks

- [useContainer](/docs/usecontainer--docs): A hook to track the width of a container element.
- [useId](/docs/useid--docs): A hook to create id from an optional id.
- [useMedia](/docs/usemedia--docs): A hook to respond to CSS media query changes in React components.

hooks-usestyleoverride--docs.content:
import { Canvas, Controls, Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import * as Stories from "./useStyleOverride.stories";

<Meta title="hooks/useStyleOverride" />

# useStyleOverride

The `useStyleOverride` hook allows you to generate new token values and apply them to a part of your page.

## API

`useStyleOverride` accept an object with the provided tokens to override as key-value pairs as a first argument.
The second argument is an optional string to target a specific element. It's passed to the `document.querySelector` method.

```ts
import { useStyleOverride } from "@qlik/sprout-react-hooks";
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Default} />
<Controls of={Stories.Default} />
